title: What are coping strategies?
citation: Folkman, S., & Lazarus, R.S. (1988). Coping as a mediator of emotion. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 54(3), 466-475.
content:
Coping strategies are the thoughts and behaviours people use to manage the internal and external demands of situations that are appraised as stressful. The term covers a wide range of responses, from deliberate problem-solving to unconscious habits, and from reaching out to others to withdrawing from a situation entirely. Coping is not a single act but an ongoing process that shifts as circumstances change and as our appraisal of those circumstances changes.

Researchers distinguish between adaptive and maladaptive coping. Adaptive coping strategies are those that tend to reduce stress or distress over time without creating significant new problems. They include reframing a situation in a more balanced way, breaking a problem into manageable steps, seeking social support, engaging in physical activity, or practising relaxation techniques. Maladaptive coping strategies may offer short-term relief but tend to maintain or worsen distress in the long run. Common examples include avoiding situations that trigger anxiety, suppressing unwanted thoughts, using alcohol or substances to numb difficult feelings, or ruminating on problems without taking action.

The boundary between adaptive and maladaptive coping is not always sharp. Rest and distraction, for example, can serve a genuinely restorative function in small doses, but become avoidance when they prevent someone from addressing a situation that needs attention. Context matters. What works well in one type of stressor may be less useful in another.

One of the most important insights from coping research is that coping is a skill, not a fixed trait. People are not born as good or poor copers in an unchangeable sense. The strategies a person learns, practises, and refines over time shape how effectively they navigate stress. This means that coping capacity can be strengthened through learning and deliberate practice, a finding that underlies many evidence-based psychological interventions.
